coredata.dc:title: Pyrolysis oil to power: Carbon nanomaterials as photosensitizers for high-performance Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells

coredata.dc:description: Pyrolysis oil, a byproduct of biomass pyrolysis, has faced significant challenges due to its high acidity, complex composition, and low heating value, limiting its direct application. This study presents an innovative approach to valorize cornstalk-derived pyrolysis oil by transforming it into advanced carbon-based nanomaterials for Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSCs). Through atmospheric distillation, pyrolysis oil was converted into bio-oil-derived char, featuring a hybrid structure of graphitic (sp2) and disordered (sp3) domains enriched with oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. This char served as a sustainable precursor for nanocarbon production (BCDs). Two types of carbon nanomaterials were synthesized: Carbon Quantum Dots via N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solvothermal treatment, and Carbon Nanodots via hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) oxidation. BCDs (DMF) exhibited a crystalline graphitic structure, green fluorescence, a high quantum yield (19.9 %), and a narrow band gap (2.2 eV). In contrast, BCDs (H2O2) displayed an amorphous structure, blue fluorescence, a lower quantum yield (9 %), and a wider band gap (2.8 eV). When applied as photosensitizers in DSSCs, BCDs (DMF) achieved a remarkable 59.2 % increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) and a 47.7 % enhancement in short-circuit current density (Jsc) compared to BCDs(H2O2). The superior performance of BCDs (DMF) is attributed to their enhanced light absorption, lower recombination rates (keff), extended electron lifetimes (τn), faster electron transport times (τs), and more efficient charge transfer (Rct). This study demonstrates a sustainable strategy for converting pyrolysis oil into functional nanomaterials, unlocking new opportunities for biomass-based clean energy applications.

head.ce:title: Pyrolysis oil to power: Carbon nanomaterials as photosensitizers for high-performance Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Shipping and Carbon Neutrality Laboratory, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Green Shipping and Carbon Neutrality Laboratory
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Shipping and Carbon Neutrality Laboratory, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pyrolysis oil, a byproduct of biomass pyrolysis, has faced significant challenges due to its high acidity, complex composition, and low heating value, limiting its direct application. This study presents an innovative approach to valorize cornstalk-derived pyrolysis oil by transforming it into advanced carbon-based nanomaterials for Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSCs). Through atmospheric distillation, pyrolysis oil was converted into bio-oil-derived char, featuring a hybrid structure of graphitic (sp2) and disordered (sp3) domains enriched with oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. This char served as a sustainable precursor for nanocarbon production (BCDs). Two types of carbon nanomaterials were synthesized: Carbon Quantum Dots via N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solvothermal treatment, and Carbon Nanodots via hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) oxidation. BCDs (DMF) exhibited a crystalline graphitic structure, green fluorescence, a high quantum yield (19.9 %), and a narrow band gap (2.2 eV). In contrast, BCDs (H2O2) displayed an amorphous structure, blue fluorescence, a lower quantum yield (9 %), and a wider band gap (2.8 eV). When applied as photosensitizers in DSSCs, BCDs (DMF) achieved a remarkable 59.2 % increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) and a 47.7 % enhancement in short-circuit current density (Jsc) compared to BCDs(H2O2). The superior performance of BCDs (DMF) is attributed to their enhanced light absorption, lower recombination rates (keff), extended electron lifetimes (τn), faster electron transport times (τs), and more efficient charge transfer (Rct). This study demonstrates a sustainable strategy for converting pyrolysis oil into functional nanomaterials, unlocking new opportunities for biomass-based clean energy applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The rising global energy demand, driven by population growth and industrialization, necessitates sustainable alternatives to fossil fuels. Renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, and hydrogen are essential for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and combating climate change. Among these, solar energy stands out as a key solution due to its abundance and scalability (Singh, 2013). Silicon solar cells were initially considered promising for their ability to harvest photons efficiently. However, their manufacturing cost, efficiency, and material quality challenges have limited their widespread adoption (Andreani et al., 2019). In contrast, Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), a third-generation photovoltaic technology, offer an innovative alternative due to their affordability, flexibility, and impressive photoconversion efficiency (John Peter et al., 2021; Rahman et al., 2023; Rezaei et al., 2019a). First discovered by O'Regan and Grätzel in 1991, Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs) mimic photosynthesis in plants, making them an attractive option for sustainable energy (Anandkumar and Rajakumar, 2018). In Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), the photosensitizers or sensitizing dye are critical for capturing light and producing electron-hole pairs, which are fundamental for generating electrical energy (He et al., 2016).

Traditional sensitizing dyes, such as ruthenium-based complexes such as N3 ((cis-bis (isothiocyanato) bis (2, 2′-bipyridyl-4, 4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II)), N719 (Di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis (isothiocyanato) bis (2, 2′bipyridyl-4, 4′dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II)), and N749 or black dye (Tris (N, N, N-tributyl-1-butanaminium) [[2,2″6′,2″-terpyridine]-4,4′,4″-tricarboxylato(3-)-N1, N1′, N1′′] tris (thiocyanato-N) hydrogen ruthenate) have been widely used due to their high efficiency. However, these materials face significant challenges, including high costs, potential toxicity, and environmental risks associated with their synthesis and disposal (Bora et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2020a, 2020b; Noman et al., 2022; Riaz et al., 2019; Tomar et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2019). These limitations have spurred research into alternative dyes, such as metal-free organic dyes, natural dyes, and quantum dots. While these alternatives offer advantages in terms of sustainability and cost-effectiveness, they often struggle with stability, efficiency, and scalability issues for large-scale production ( Liu et al., 2020a,b).

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained attention as a promising material for enhancing the performance of Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs). With their zero-dimensional structure, carbon core, and diverse functional groups, CDs exhibit exceptional photoluminescence, tunable optical properties, and efficient electron transfer capabilities (Bora et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2025; Gao et al., 2020a, 2020b; Rezaei et al., 2019b; Shejale et al., 2021a, 2021b; Zhang et al., 2025). The carbon dots family, which includes Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs), Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs), and Carbon Nanodots (CNDs), can be derived from various raw carbon sources and tailored for specific applications (J. Liu et al., 2020; Molaei, 2019). In DSSCs, carbon nanodots have been used as sensitizers, co-sensitizers, and layers in photoanodes and counter-electrodes, demonstrating their potential to improve light absorption, charge transfer efficiency, and overall power conversion efficiency (Chen et al., 2025; Shejale et al., 2021b).

Although extensive research has explored various carbon precursors for carbon dots production such as carbon black, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and biomass residues (Gao et al., 2020a; Shejale et al., 2021a), the search for novel carbon sources remains crucial for advancing Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) technology. Pyrolysis oil, a byproduct of biomass pyrolysis, is a potential alternative fuel for industries, power generation, transportation, and chemical production. However, its high acidity, higher water content, complex composition, and low heating value limit direct applications (Al-Rumaihi et al., 2022; Modak et al., 2023). While upgrading techniques like emulsification, esterification, hydrogenation, and hydrodeoxygenation have enhanced its quality, cost-effective solutions remain challenging (Alvarez-Chavez et al., 2019). Therefore, exploring innovative and cost-effective applications of pyrolysis oil beyond its traditional uses is crucial. For the first time, this study introduces a transformative strategy to address these limitations by repurposing pyrolysis oil into high-performance carbon nanomaterials for renewable solar energy applications such as Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC).

The study presents an innovative approach for deriving biochar from cornstalk pyrolysis oil through a distillation process, which is then used to synthesize carbon nanodots (BCDs) via two distinct methods: oxidation with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and solvothermal synthesis with N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The resulting BCDs exhibit distinct fluorescence emissions under 365 nm: blue for the H2O2 oxidation method and green for the DMF solvothermal synthesis. These bio-oil-derived nanocarbon dots show significant variations in their surface functional groups, structural properties, and performance in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) applications. Our work demonstrates the potential of pyrolysis oil as a sustainable carbon source, offering new insights into how pyrolysis oil-derived carbon nanomaterials can enhance light absorption, charge transfer efficiency, and overall power conversion efficiency (PCE) in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs). This study bridges the gap between biomass waste utilization and advanced solar energy technologies, paving the way for cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and high-performance DSSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental and materials
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Dried Cornstalk, Titanium dioxide (TiO2 -P25), FTO slide glasses (8 Ω>), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Ferric chloride (FeCl3), Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O), Cobalt(II) chloride (CoCl2), Zinc Chloride (ZnCl2), Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), Sodium chloride (NaCl), Manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), Iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), Nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), Lead(II) chloride (PbCl2), Potassium carbonate (K2CO3), Aluminum chloride (AlCl3), Mercuric chloride (HgCl2), Chromium (III) chloride (CrCl3), Titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4), Iodine base electrolyte solution (GBO-I), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2-30 %), H2PtCl6-99 %, ethylcellulose, triphenyl, acetic acid, 22 μm polytetrafluorethylene membrane, and Ethanol were purchased and used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of cornstalk pyrolysis oil-based char
ce:section.ce:para: Producing char from bio-oil involves conducting slow pyrolysis of cornstalk at a temperature of 550 °C to generate bio-oil. The 400g of dried cornstalk is subjected to heat at 550 °C for 1 h using a pyrolysis reactor, leading to the production of 95 mL of bio-oil and 360g of biochar. Char derived from bio-oil is acquired by means of atmospheric distillation of bio-oil at room temperature. During this process, the bio-oil is distilled at a temperature of 180 °C (the temperature of the distillate) and is condensed at higher temperatures until only the residue remains. This residue is converted into solid black fuel, which is subsequently ground, sieved, dried, and stored for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (BCDs) with H2O2 oxidation method
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of cornstalk bio-oil derived char with 20 mL of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2-30 %) and 80 mL of distilled water. The mixture underwent ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and was continuously stirred for 24 h at room temperature to ensure a uniform solution. Following this, the resulting mixture was heated to 80 °C for 2 h, with the temperature being monitored using a thermometer inserted into the solution. Following thermal treatment, the reaction mixture was cooled and diluted to a final volume of 100 mL with distilled water. Purification was achieved through a multi-step process. Initially, centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min was performed to remove aggregates, and the supernatant was retained. Subsequently, the supernatant was sterile-filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate residual particulates. To remove residual impurities, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da for 24 h. The purified solution was then lyophilized for 48 h to obtain the BCDs (H2O2) powder, which was prepared for subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (BCDs) with DMF solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of cornstalk bio-oil derived char was added to 40 mL of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), followed by the addition of 10 mL of distilled water. The mixture was then treated with ultrasound for 1 h and continuously stirred at room temperature to ensure a uniform solution. The resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 8 h. The BCDs (DMF) solution mixture was subsequently dried using a rotary evaporator. The BCDs (DMF) solid was reconstituted in 40 mL of deionized water. To remove insoluble particulates, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then sterile-filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Dialysis was performed against deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1000 Da for 24 h, ensuring thorough removal of residual impurities. Finally, the purified solution was subjected to lyophilization, yielding the BCDs (DMF) sample, which was collected for subsequent analytical characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of stability tests for BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2)
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) was assessed via fluorescence spectroscopy in aqueous solutions containing 10 μM of various metal ions (Cr3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+). For each metal ion, 2 mL of BCDs (DMF) or BCDs (H2O2) solution was added to the metal ion solution. pH stability was evaluated across a pH range of 1–13. The solutions were prepared using deionized water, with the pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13) adjusted by adding 0.1 M HCl for acidic conditions and 0.1 M NaOH for alkaline conditions. Temperature stability was determined by incubating BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) solutions at temperatures from 10 °C to 90 °C for 30 min. Fluorescence intensity changes were recorded to quantify the stability of the BCD solutions under each condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing the photoanode for DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTO slide glass, initially 1.6 cm × 1.6 cm in size with a resistance of less than 8 Ω, was first cleaned with deionized water and then with ethanol for 10 min each. After cleaning, the slide was treated with TiCl4 for 30 min at 70 °C and washed with ethanol. The photoanode was then prepared using screen-printing to control the semiconductor TiO2 paste thickness to 13–15 μm. The paste was composed of TiO2-P25, ethylcellulose, alpha-triphenol, acetic acid, and ethanol in a weight ratio of 2.1:1:2:0.6:0.36, providing an active area of 0.19 cm2. After each coating, the photoanode was sintered at 80 °C for about 15 min, then gradually heated to 500 °C for 30 min. Finally, a post-TiCl4 treatment was applied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of photosensitizer and fabrication of solar cell devices
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared photoanodes were submerged in the prepared bio-oil-derived carbon nanodots, BCDs (H2O2), and BCDs (DMF) for 24 h to ensure optimal carbon dot absorption. After the carbon nano-loading process, the photoanodes were rinsed thoroughly with ethanol and then left to air-dry. The prepared Pt-coating film was used for the counter electrode. The assembly of the DSSC involved sandwiching the photoanode and counter electrode and injecting an iodide/triiodide base electrolyte into the device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization techniques of solid fuel, nanocarbon, and DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition of carbon (C), hydrogen (H), and nitrogen (N) in the bio-oil-derived char and bio-oil samples was analyzed using an elemental analyzer (Elemental Unicube, Germany). Calorific values were determined with a calorimeter (SJLRY-8000, China). The fixed carbon (FC) and volatile matter (VM) content were evaluated through proximate analysis, adhering to the national standards GB/T 212 2008 and GB/T 213 2008 of China. The bio-oil composition was analyzed using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS, GC-7890A). The morphology was examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8100, Japan). Raman spectroscopy was performed on samples using the Horiba Lab RAM HR Evolution (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis used a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system (USA). Optical absorption measurements were conducted with a JINGHUA-754 PC/UV–VIS spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence emission of carbon nanodots was quantified using the F97-Pro photo-spectrometer.

The PL intensities of fabricated DSSCs were tested with a photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). The morphology of carbon nanodots was examined with a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 instrument operating at 200 kV. Chemical interactions in bio-oil derived char and the surface functional groups of carbon dots were examined with a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Bruker Alpha-T). XRD analysis of carbon dots was tested with X-ray diffractometer (Germany Bruker D8 Advance). I-V measurements and performance of DSSC were conducted under an illumination intensity of 100 mW/cm2 using a Solar Simulator (PEC-L15, Japan). All electrochemical analyses were performed using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Bio oil compositions
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Table S (1), the composition of bio-oil derived from cornstalk reveals a diverse array of organic compounds, including acids, esters, aldehydes, furans, phenols, ketones, and other unidentified components. This complex mixture underscores the high oxygen content characteristic of cornstalk bio-oil, which is enriched with both aliphatic and aromatic compounds. Such a composition highlights its potential as an organic-rich, carbon-intensive solid fuel. Additionally, the bio-oil obtained through slow pyrolysis comprises both light and heavy oil fractions, accompanied by a significant water content. Notably, the aqueous phase constitutes 65 % of the bio-oil, while the remaining 35 % represents the oil phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultimate and proximate properties of bio-oil-derived char
ce:section.ce:para: After cornstalk bio-oil distillation, bio-oil-derived char (bio-oil distillation residue) can be produced as the carbon source for carbon dots precursors, similar to commercial solid fuel char (Chong et al., 2023), as shown in Fig. (1) . The bio-oil-derived char analysis revealed higher carbon and hydrogen concentrations and lower oxygen levels compared to the raw bio-oil, as shown in Table 1 . This means that some of the light-oxygenated groups of the bio-oil were removed during the distillation process. Due to its high carbon content, bio-oil-derived char can serve as a raw carbon source for the synthesis of carbon-based materials.

After bio-oil distillation, the distilled residue of cornstalk exhibited an increase in carbon content from 40.11 % to 71.72 %. Compared to the hydrogen (H) content of primary bio-oil, the H content of char increased from 5.86 % to 6.16 %. In contrast to the oxygen content of bio-oil, the oxygen (O) content of the char decreased from 53.11 % to 19.01 %. Thus, the resulting sold fuel char is similar to traditional solid fuel in that it has a high content of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, as well as trace amounts of sulfur and nitrogen (Saikia et al., 2022).

The Higher Heating Value (HHV) of bio-oil-derived char is a key indicator of its quality as a solid fuel. The char produced from cornstalk bio-oil exhibits an HHV of 27.48 MJ/kg, significantly higher than that of the raw bio-oil (12.57 MJ/kg) and comparable to many commercial solid fuels (Cheng et al., 2020; Shao et al., 2023). This elevated HHV, coupled with high carbon content and favorable oxygen/nitrogen levels, further establishes bio-oil-derived char as an excellent precursor for synthesizing carbon nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and structural characteristics of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2)
Observation from electron beam analysis (TEM/HRTEM)
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis demonstrated that both Bio oil-derived carbon dots (BCDs) synthesized using DMF and those treated with H2O2 displayed a quasi-spherical morphology, with average particle sizes of 3.1 nm and 4.4 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). This suggests that the synthesis of carbon nanodots using DMF tends to yield smaller nanoparticles compared to those produced with H2O2. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images in Fig. 2(c) and (d) display distinct lattice patterns in the BCDs synthesized with DMF and H2O2. The observed lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating a crystalline graphitic structure. In contrast, the 0.22 nm spacing likely corresponds to the (100) plane, characteristic of graphitic carbon or graphene-like domains (Li et al., 2019; Pal et al., 2020; Setianto et al., 2024). Both BCDs (DMF) spacings indicate a combination of crystalline graphitic regions (Pal et al., 2020).In contrast, the BCDs synthesized with H2O2 displayed no crystalline structure, resulting in a more amorphous form. HRTEM analysis revealed that the BCDs produced in DMF and H2O2 differed in size and crystalline domain characteristics due to variations in solvent and synthesis conditions. The use of DMF as a solvent in the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from pyrolysis oil promotes the formation of smaller nanoparticles with more distinct crystalline graphitic domains. Conversely, the BCDs synthesized in H2O2 resulted in larger particles with an amorphous structure.

Therefore, the BCDs produced in DMF exhibited a well-defined Carbon Quantum dot nature (CQDs), while those synthesized with H2O2 demonstrated more characteristics of carbon nanodots (CDs). These differences highlight the significant influence of synthesis parameters on the structural and electronic properties of bio-oil-derived carbon nanodots. A comprehensive analysis of XRD, XPS, and their optical properties will offer deeper insights into the unique characteristics of carbon dots in BCDs. Generally, Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) that possess crystalline graphite structures or graphene-like features can significantly enhance electron and charge transfer in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) systems (Chou et al., 2023; Mahalingam et al., 2021; Shejale et al., 2021c). This improvement in transfer can lead to enhanced overall performance of DSSC, ultimately boosting solar cell efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from XPS, FTIR, and Raman Analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of carbon dots (BCDs) synthesized using N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) revealed three distinct peaks corresponding to carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s) at binding energies of 284.31 eV, 284.91 eV, and 532.23 eV for BCDs.

(H2O2), 297.9 eV, 297.9 eV, and 544.9 eV for BCDs (DMF). In terms of atomic percentages, BCDs (DMF) comprised carbon (76 %), oxygen (17.4 %), and nitrogen (6.6 %), while BCDs (H2O2) contained carbon (37.3 %), oxygen (60.9 %), and nitrogen (1.8 %), as shown in Figs. 3(a) and 4(a)
. High-resolution spectra of these elements are shown in Fig. 3(b–d) and Fig. 4(b–d). Furthermore, Table 2 presents a comparative analysis of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) compositional properties. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum for BCDs (DMF) confirmed the presence of C=C/C-C bonds at 284.34 eV, indicative of sp2 carbon and suggestive of a graphitic carbon domain.

The sp3 carbon bonds (C-C/C-H) are observed at 285.0 eV. BCDs (DMF) exhibited a higher sp2 carbon content (38.4 %) compared to sp3 carbon (16.8 %), which reflects a well-defined crystalline structure. Furthermore, oxygen-functionalized groups were detected, with peaks at 286.2 eV (C-O) and 288.2 eV (C=O/C-OH), indicating the surface functionalization of BCDs (DMF) that can improve their hydrophilicity. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum of BCDs in DMF reveals two distinct groups: C=O and C=O-C=O at 532.1 eV and C-OH/C-O-C groups at 533.1 eV. Similarly, the high-resolution N1s spectrum of BCDs in DMF indicates the presence of pyridinic N (=C-N-C) at 398.7 eV, pyrrolic N (C-N-C) at 400.6 eV, and graphitic N or amine N at 400.0 eV (Cao et al., 2022).

The deconvoluted C1s spectra of BCDs (H2O2) confirmed the presence of various carbon bonds. The C=C bond was observed at 284.6 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon, while the C-C/C-H bonds appeared at 285.2 eV, indicating sp3 hybridization. Additional features included the C-O bond at 286.4 eV and the C=O bond at 288.8 eV. Similarly, the high-resolution O1s spectra of BCDs (H2O2) confirmed the presence of the C=O bond at 532.2 eV and C-OH/C-O-C bonds at 532.7 eV. Furthermore, the high-resolution N1s spectra showed notable peaks for pyridinic C=N-C at 398.7 eV, pyrrolic N of C-N-C at 400.7 eV, and graphitic N or amine N-H bonds at 400.1 eV. In BCDs (H2O2), the higher sp3 carbon content (27.8 %) compared to sp2 carbon content (25.8 %) which reflects the morphology of the carbon dots, leading to a more amorphous structure than that of BCDs (DMF).

In this study, the nitrogen present in the bio-oil-derived char precursor is directly incorporated into the carbon dots during synthesis, leading to natural nitrogen self-doping. This eliminates the need for external nitrogen sources in the synthesized process (Park et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2014). However, solvent choice significantly influenced nitrogen incorporation. Due to its intrinsic nitrogen content, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) facilitated a higher degree of nitrogen doping, particularly in the form of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen (Kao et al., 2016; Mazzucato et al., 2023). Thus, XPS analysis demonstrated that carbon dots (BCDs) synthesized with N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) exhibited a significantly higher nitrogen content compared to those synthesized with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). This increase is attributed to the solvothermal effect of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), which facilitates enhanced nitrogen doping (6.6 %), specifically increasing the graphitic nitrogen content on the carbon dot surface (see Table 2). Conversely, BCDs synthesized using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) displayed a higher oxygen content, as evidenced by varying atomic concentrations. Enhanced this nitrogen doping improved crystallinity, light absorption, and stability, optimizing the electronic properties and charge transport capabilities of the carbon dots (Gao et al., 2019; Kao et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2020a). As a result, the nitrogen doping effect of BCDs (DMF) demonstrated superior performance in Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSCs), attributed to improved charge separation and transport efficiency (Gao et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2020b; Shahid et al., 2023a, 2023b).

The FTIR analysis revealed various functional groups and chemical bonds in both bio-oil-based char and bio-oil, along with their synthesized carbon dots (BCDs) using H2O2 and DMF, which are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The FTIR observation of bio-oil and bio-oil base char indicated that there are oxygenated species, such as alcohol groups, are present around 3410 cm−1, which relates to O-H stretching vibrations. Additionally, carbonyl groups (C=O) showed at about 1855 cm−1, while alkenes or aromatic compounds (C=C) appear at 1654 cm−1. Aromatic and aliphatic hydrocarbon groups appeared at about 3135 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1. The FTIR absorption bands between 983 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1 are associated with C-O bond strength in phenol, methyl (C-H) bending, and O-H bending in phenol, as well as other related vibrations present in both bio-oil and bio-oil-based char. Additionally, the FTIR peaks at 1560 cm−1 and 1760 cm−1 are attributed to C=O, C=C, and C=N stretching vibrations found in both bio-oil and bio-oil-based char. The FTIR results of bio-oil and raw bio-oil base char suggest that they are rich in a variety of organic compounds, oxygen-containing groups, and some amount of nitrogen groups. In contrast, biochar contains fewer volatile compounds and a higher proportion of stable compounds compared to raw bio-oil. This is due to the polymerization and condensation reactions that occur during the distillation process. These transformations enrich biochar with a diverse range of functional groups such as nitrogen and oxygen, making it a promising material for further applications. Thus, the distillate residue of bio-oil (biochar) is particularly suitable as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon nanodots, offering a sustainable and efficient route for nanomaterial production.

The FTIR spectrum also shows various surface functional groups in carbon nanodots (BCDs) prepared with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and dimethylformamide (DMF). The absorption bands between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 indicate stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups. Aliphatic C-H stretching vibrations occurred around 2833 cm−1 for BCDs (H2O2) and at 2835 cm−1 and 2940 cm−1 for BCDs (DMF). Bands between 1565 cm−1 and 1768 cm−1 correspond to C=O, C=C, and C=N stretching vibrations which showed in both functional groups of BCDs (Aung et al., 2024). The bands between 1210 cm−1 and 1525 cm−1 also relate to C-N, C-O, and C-H bond stretching. The FTIR analysis reveals that both H2O2 andDMF methods introduce different functional groups onto the carbon nanodots, including hydroxyl, amine, carbonyl, and aromatic groups.

Thus, FTIR analysis confirmed the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups into both BCDs synthesized with H2O2 and DMF. However, BCDs synthesized with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) showed a higher concentration of nitrogen-functional groups (C=N, N-H, and C-N stretches), indicating a stronger nitrogen doping effect. This enhancement is due to the solvent effects, with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), a nitrogen-containing solvent, promoting greater nitrogen incorporation during synthesis. The presence of these nitrogen-functional groups modifies the electronic structure of the carbon dots, creating additional energy states that improve photon absorption and electron excitation (dos Santos de Almeida et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2017a; Vu Nhat et al., 2024). Nitrogen doping is crucial for enhancing light-harvesting capabilities, stability, and optical properties (Liu et al., 2017b; Manioudakis et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2017). Consequently, BCDs (DMF) with higher nitrogen content exhibit superior light absorption and performance compared to BCDs (H2O2), which have higher oxygen content (Manioudakis et al., 2019, 2019; Yang et al., 2014). Thus, nitrogen doping in BCDs (DMF) leads to better charge transfer efficiency, stability, and overall DSSC performance (Anandkumar and Rajakumar, 2018; Kundu et al., 2017; Rao et al., n.d.).

In the Raman analysis of bio-oil-based char, BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) exhibited two prominent peaks in the spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5(b). At approximately 1372 cm−1, the first peak corresponds to the D band associated with aromatic rings and carbon atoms in sp3 hybridization. The second peak, observed at 1575 cm−1, aligns with the G band, indicating graphitic structures, highlighting sp2 hybridized carbon (Xu et al., 2021). The D to G band intensities (ID/IG) ratio in bio-oil-based char is found to be 0.94, indicating an amorphous structure that is a hybrid of materials containing graphitic (sp2) and disordered (fl) characteristics which has a stable carbon-rich structure with fewer volatile components which is suitable for precursors for carbon nano-based materials.

The ID/IG ratio of BCDs synthesized with DMF is 0.86, indicating a lower level of disorder compared to BCDs synthesized with H2O2, which have an ID/IG ratio of 1.03. This suggests that the solvothermal synthesis of BCDs using DMF promotes a higher degree of graphitization, resulting in fewer structural defects in the carbon nanodot structure (Mintz et al., 2020). Conversely, the H2O2 oxidation method produces a more defect-rich, amorphous structure, as observed in the HRTEM and XPS analyses.

Fig. 5(c) and d showed the broad diffraction pattern observed at around 28° in the XRD analysis of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) is associated with the (002) plane in the graphite lattice, suggesting the presence of disordered or amorphous carbon atoms within the structures of all BCDs. Furthermore, there is a noticeable peak at 43° in BCDs (DMF), which corresponds to the (100) plane, indicating a certain level of crystallinity in this sample (Y. Liu et al., 2020; Mintz et al., 2020; Rodríguez-Padrón et al., 2018) Therefore, the XRD analysis indicates that the carbon nanostructures in BCDs (H2O2) are amorphous, while BCDs (DMF) exhibit a more ordered and crystalline nature. This finding is additionally observed by HRTEM and Raman analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2)
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the synthesized carbon dots were evaluated through UV–Vis absorption, Fluorescence (FL) excitation, and emission spectra. The Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of both BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2), recorded at different excitation wavelengths, are presented in Fig.(a) and (b). The emission spectra of all carbon dots display distinct characteristics, indicating an excitation-dependent emission wavelength behavior. BCDs (DMF) show the highest emission intensities between 450 nm and 750 nm when excited at 410 nm, while BCDs (H2O2) exhibit their peak emission intensity in the range of 320 nm–600 nm when excited at 320 nm. BCDs synthesized with DMF emit green fluorescence, while those synthesized with H2O2 emit blue fluorescence. This variation in fluorescence is ascribed to the recombination of excited electrons resulting from the n-π∗ transition. This phenomenon is evident in the sensing of heavy metals, where interactions between carbon dots and the metals lead to quenching effects and shifts in UV–vis transitions, as observed in previous studies (Aung et al., 2024).

The photoluminescence of carbon dots (CDs) is governed by electronic transitions within the carbon core and surface states, which, along with their structure and functionalization, determine their fluorescence properties(Koç et al., 2022; Tiwari et al., 2023). The quantum yield (QY%) represents the ratio of emitted to absorbed photons, which is a key indicator of their fluorescence efficiency and ability to convert absorbed light into emitted light(Khan et al., 2017; Nawara and Waluk, 2017). In this study, the Quantum Yield of BCDs (H2O2) is 9 % when excited at 320 nm, and the Quantum Yield of BCDs (DMF) is 19.3 % at 410 nm of excitation wavelength. These values were calculated using the relative method (Fig.S.1 and Fig.S.2). Thus, BCDs (DMF) exhibit a higher Quantum Yield (QY%) than BCDs (H2O2).

BCDs (DMF), synthesized via a solvothermal process, possess a crystalline graphitic structure with significant nitrogen doping (6.6 %), as confirmed by TEM and XPS analyses. The incorporation of nitrogen creates additional energy states within the bandgap, reducing the energy gap to 2.2 eV (Fig. 6 d). This narrowing enhances photon absorption and fluorescence emission efficiency (Nguyen et al., 2022a). FTIR spectra also show strong C-N, C=N, and N-H stretching peaks in BCDs (DMF), indicating successful nitrogen incorporation. In contrast, these peaks are weaker or absent in BCDs (H2O2), suggesting lower nitrogen content. The higher nitrogen content in BCDs (DMF) helps reduce nonradiative electron recombination, prolongs the excited-state lifetime, and improves quantum yield by stabilizing surface states and passivating defects (Koç et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2022a, 2022b). Additionally, the crystalline structure of BCDs (DMF), with its higher sp2 content, promotes efficient electron delocalization across the carbon core (Wang et al., 2021) and reduces energy barriers for charge transport, further enhancing their superior fluorescence emission properties (Sengottuvelu et al., 2022). This combination of nitrogen doping, surface passivation, improved electron delocalization, and structural order ultimately boosts the quantum yield of BCDs (DMF) compared to the more disordered BCDs (H2O2). Thus, the synthesis method, precursor material, and solvent type are crucial factors in determining the structure and properties of carbon dots, which in turn influence their fluorescence emission.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the carbon dots generally exhibit two absorption peaks, as shown in Fig. 6(c). BCDs synthesized with H2O2 exhibit a peak around 233 nm, whereas BCDs synthesized with DMF show a peak at 265 nm. These peaks are attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 carbon core (C=C bonds). Additionally, a second peak is observed around 320 nm for BCDs (H2O2) and 362 nm for BCDs (DMF), which corresponds to oxygen or nitrogen-containing carbon functional groups (e.g., carbonyl or amine groups) and the n-π∗ transition resulting from surface modifications (Das et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2021). The BCDs synthesized with DMF exhibit stronger and broader absorbance in the visible range than those synthesized with H2O2, indicating enhanced light-harvesting capabilities. This distinction arises from differences in synthesis methods and solvent effects. In the UV–Vis spectra, the absorbance peaks associated with π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions in BCDs (DMF) show a red-shift relative to BCDs (H2O2). This red shift results from the incorporation of nitrogen-containing functional groups from the DMF solvent, which increases the nitrogen heteroatom content on the carbon dots' surface. This surface modification enhances conjugation within the carbon core and the electron-donating properties of the BCDs, leading to the observed optical shift (Bhattacharyya et al., 2017). The band gap values of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2), as shown in Fig. 6(d), are calculated by the Tauc Plot equation (1).

Where h is Planck's constant, v is the incident light frequency, Eg is the energy bandgap, A is a proportionality constant, α is the absorption coefficient, and n is a component (direct transition 2, for indirect is ½).

BCDs (DMF) exhibit a lower band gap of 2.2 eV, while BCDs (H2O2) have a higher band gap of 2.8 eV. Notably, the lower band gap of BCDs (DMF) is associated with green fluorescence emission, whereas the larger band gap of BCDs (H2O2) corresponds to blue fluorescence emission. The reduced band gap in BCDs (DMF) is attributed to their higher absorption and red-shift in absorbance, likely resulting from changes in surface functional groups in BCDs (DMF) compared to those synthesized using H2O2, as observed in the UV–Vis, XPS, FL, and FTIR studies mentioned above. The electronic properties and fluorescence emission of carbon nanodots are strongly influenced by surface modifications, particularly the incorporation of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and fluorine, which can lead to the creation of new energy levels and a red-shift in the emission peaks (Li et al., 2023; Ren et al., 2022).

In this study, the high concentration of nitrogen atoms on the surface of BCDs (DMF) introduces localized electronic states and enhances π-conjugation. This surface modification improves light absorption compared to BCDs (H2O2) by narrowing the bandgap and facilitating electron transitions, as nitrogen effectively alters the π-electron system and surface structure (Li et al., 2023; Ren et al., 2022). Thus, the nitrogen-rich functional groups in BCDs (DMF) contribute to a lower bandgap, enhancing light-harvesting capabilities and improving the performance of Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSCs). In contrast, the oxygen-rich functional groups in BCDs (H2O2) are associated with a higher bandgap, limiting light absorption and resulting in comparatively lower DSSC performance. This highlights that the surface modification of bio-oil-derived carbon nanodots plays a crucial role in determining their light-harvesting efficiency and structural properties, significantly impacting the overall efficiency of the DSSC system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2)
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we examined the stability of the synthesized BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) concerning pH, temperature, and resistance to ionic solutions. The results of these tests provide additional insights into the functional and structural stability of BCDs under various environmental conditions.

pH stability: The pH stability of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) was evaluated across a wide pH range (1–13) by monitoring fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), both BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) maintained relatively stable fluorescence between pH 1 and 11, indicating that their optical properties remain largely unaffected under mild to moderate pH conditions. However, a significant quenching of fluorescence was observed at pH 13. This quenching likely results from the deprotonation on the surface of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2), where the amine groups act as strong electron-donor functional groups in highly alkaline conditions (Filippini et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2024; Zuo et al., 2023). Notably, BCDs (DMF) displayed more excellent pH resistance than BCDs (H2O2), possibly due to nitrogen-rich functional groups enhancing their stability.

Thermal Stability: The thermal stability of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) was evaluated by measuring their fluorescence intensity over a temperature range of 10 °C–90 °C for 30 min at each temperature. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of both BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) remained unchanged up to 30 °C. However, as shown in Fig. 7(b), both types of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) exhibited a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity as the temperature increased beyond 30 °C. This indicates that they undergo lower thermal-induced quenching. Notably, BCDs (DMF) displayed higher fluorescence intensity at elevated temperatures compared to BCDs (H2O2), indicating higher thermal stability. This improved stability of BCDs (DMF) can be attributed to their well-defined graphitic domains and nitrogen doping (Dua et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2017c, 2023). These features enhance sp2 and sp3 bonding (Nguyen et al., 2022c) and provide surface passivation (Manioudakis et al., 2019) to the carbon dots. As a result, this structure becomes less flexible and minimizes fluorescence quenching even at high temperatures.

Ionic Stability: The stability of BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) in the presence of various metal ions (Cr3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Hg2+) was assessed to evaluate their potential interactions with different ionic species. The fluorescence stability of both BCDs in various metal ion solutions, as shown in 7(c) and (d), highlights their response to ion-rich conditions. BCDs (DMF) demonstrated a strong fluorescence quenching effect when exposed to Cu2+ ions, indicating significant coordination interactions between the copper ions and their surface functional groups, particularly nitrogen-containing functional groups such as amino groups (Rahimi et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2018). In contrast, BCDs (H2O2) also showed notable fluorescence quenching when interacting with Fe3+ ions with oxygen-containing functional groups on the BCD surface (Aung et al., 2024; Phan et al., 2022), highlighting their high sensitivity to iron ions. These findings suggest that BCDs (DMF) and (H2O2) have the potential to act as selective fluorescent sensors for detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. The stability assessment confirms that BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2) show strong resistance to changes in pH, temperature, and ionic environments. Notably, BCDs (DMF) demonstrate superior thermal and pH stability. Additionally, the selective fluorescence quenching effects observed with Cu2+ and Fe3+ indicate their potential applications in metal ion sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of nanocarbon dots (BCDs) in DSSC
Photovoltaic and electron impedance spectroscopy performance of BCDs nanocarbon dots-based DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The different nanocarbon dots (BCDs) used in the fabrication system of Dye-Sensitized Solar cells (DSSCs) (showed in Fig. 8 (a) and (b)) were performed under standard one-sun illumination conditions (100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5 G), and current density-voltage (J-V) measurements were obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (a).

The BCDs (H2O2) based Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) demonstrated a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 0.304 mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.494 V, a fill factor (FF) of 0.658, and an overall efficiency (PCE) of 0.098 %. In contrast, the incorporation of BCDs (DMF) based DSSC device significantly improved its performance, resulting in a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 0.449 mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.513 V, a fill factor (FF) of 0.662, and an overall efficiency (PCE) of 0.156 %. These results are detailed in Table 3 .

According to the above results, the introduction of the BCDs (DMF)-based device, Jsc increased by 47.7 %, while the power conversion efficiency (PCE) improved by 59.2 % compared to the BCDs (H2O2) based DSSC. The enhanced efficiency of BCDs (DMF) based DSSCs can be attributed to several factors, including improved charge separation, faster electron transport, and reduced charge recombination. BCDs synthesized for DMF-based DSSC devices can absorb light more effectively across a broader spectrum, maximizing photo absorption in DSSCs due to surface modification with nitrogen-containing functional groups and higher light harvesting. This enhancement in performance leads to an improved power conversion efficiency (PCE %). The nitrogen functional groups present in BCDs, including pyridine nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen, and amine nitrogen, create additional energy levels between the π states of carbon and the π∗ states of oxygen. These groups enhance the absorption of UV to visible light, resulting in the generation of photo-excited electrons and promoting electron transfer to the conduction band of the semiconductor TiO2. As a result, a greater presence of nitrogen functional groups facilitates more efficient electron injection into the conduction band of the semiconductor (Gao et al., 2020a; Jahantigh et al., 2020; Younas et al., 2019).

To evaluate DSSC performance and charge dynamics, the electrochemical properties of DSSC were analyzed using Electro impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under dark conditions with a bias voltage of 0.49 V, across a frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz. The Nyquist plot revealed a semicircle at intermediate frequencies, corresponding to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) within the TiO2/BCDs/electrolyte system(Rezaei et al., 2019c). Table 4 provides detailed DSSCs impedance data.

The overall sheet resistance (Rs) of the DSSC devices ranged from 36.04 Ω to 17.08 Ω. The lower Rs value observed in the BCDs (DMF) resulted in a higher short-circuit current, which in turn led to greater power output in the Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) system compared to the BCDs (H2O2) based DSSC devices as shown in Fig. 9(b). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) for the BCDs (DMF)-based DSSC was measured at 190 Ω, showing that it has lower charge transfer resistance compared to the BCDs (H2O2), which had an Rct of 411 Ω. This demonstrates that BCDs (DMF) showed higher electron flow within the DSSC system more effectively than those generated with BCDs (H2O2), owing to the lower Rct(Mahalingam et al., 2021; Rao et al., 2024; Rezaei et al., 2019c). Thus, BCDs (DMF) significantly enhance charge carrier transport, reducing the recombination process between TiO2 and the electrolyte. The decreased value in Rct indicates a significant enhancement in charge transfer efficiency and a reduction in recombination within the BCDs-modified photoanodes (Rezaei et al., 2019c). Furthermore, electron transport parameters such as electron recombination time, electron transfer times, and electron combination rate were calculated from EIS analysis. The recombination rate was calculated using Equation (2) to assess the electron lifetimes (τn) of the DSSCs.

According to the analysis of the above equations, the DSSC configuration based on BCDs (H2O2) showed an electron lifetime (τn) of 0.96 ms and an electron transport time (τs) of 0.841 ms. In contrast, the DSSC device using BCDs (DMF) exhibited a longer electron lifetime (τn) of 1.902 ms and a higher electron transport time (τs) of 1.712 ms. This indicates that the BCDs (DMF) DSSC device significantly enhances photovoltaic efficiency by extending both electron lifetimes and transport times compared to BCDs (H2O2) based DSSCs. Generally, lower recombination processes occur when the electron lifetimes in DSSCs are longer, which benefits the overall efficiency of DSSCs (Chou et al., 2023).

The evaluated values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) from the BCDs (DMF) based DSSC displayed a decreasing result, indicating that it enhances electron injection and transport and reduces recombination. Specifically, the BCDs (DMF) based DSSC device had a lower Rct, which corresponded to a reduced recombination rate (keff = 0.917 ms−1) and resulted in higher efficiency in J-V analysis. In contrast, the BCDs (H2O2) device exhibited a higher Rct value, leading to an increased electron recombination rate (keff = 1.041 ms−1) and resulting in lower efficiency. Thus, BCD (DMF) based DSSC devices demonstrated longer electron recombination times, faster electron transport times, and lower recombination rates than those produced with BCDs (H2O2) based DSSC devices. This improvement due to BCDs (DMF) based DSSC allows more electrons to be injected into the TiO2 semiconducting layer without recombining with the dye holes in the DSSC system (Chou et al., 2023; Rao et al., 2024; Shejale et al., 2021c).

The transient photocurrent response of DSSC devices incorporating BCDs (H2O2) and BCDs (DMF) was measured under illumination for a total duration of 260 s, with alternating on/off intervals of 20 s each, as illustrated in Fig. 9(d). Six transient photocurrent cycles were conducted to evaluate the stability of the various BCD-based DSSC devices. Among these, DSSCs utilizing BCDs (DMF) exhibited higher current density and demonstrated significantly more stable photo-to-current conversion than BCDs (H2O2) based devices. This enhanced performance is attributed to the superior photon-to-current stability and stable light-harvesting capability of BCDs (DMF), which collectively contribute to the observed improvement in power conversion efficiency (PCE%) and overall device performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible DRS absorption and photoluminescence performance
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, as shown in Fig. 9(f), demonstrate the light absorption ability of DSSCs with nanocarbon dots (BCDs), synthesized using H2O2 and DMF, across wavelengths from 200 nm to 800 nm. Bare TiO2-based DSSC (without BCDs) devices exhibit the lowest absorption range. Notably, after incorporating BCDs as a dye or sensitizer in the DSSC system, the absorption rate increases compared to the bare TiO2 films. Among these, the TiO2 + BCDs (DMF) film demonstrated the highest absorption range and extended to longer wavelengths than the TiO2 +BCDs (H2O2) film. The resulting increased light absorption characteristics of BCDs (DMF) based DSSC devices due to the nitrogen-rich functional groups facilitate specific electronic transitions, which is beneficial for harvesting solar energy (Divya and Suresh, 2021). Furthermore, BCDs (DMF) exhibit a narrower bandgap energy than BCDs (H2O2); this narrow bandgap energy is essential for enhancing light absorption in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC) devices.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum resulting from electron-hole recombination in the DSSC system indicates the recombination rate. A reduced or lowest PL intensity signifies a lower recombination rate, implying enhanced charge transfer and higher performance of the DSSC device(Chou et al., 2023; Rao et al., 2024). The PL emission intensity results shown in Fig. 9. (e) reveal that the highest PL intensity is found in the BCDs (H2O2) based DSSC device, while the BCDs (DMF) based DSSC device exhibits lower PL intensities. As a result, the BCDs (DMF) based device demonstrates enhanced charge and electron transfer within the DSSC photovoltaic system, leading to improved efficiency. Thus, nitrogen-rich bio-oil-derived carbon quantum dots prove to be more beneficial for the overall performance of DSSCs compared to their oxygen-rich bio-oil- derived carbon nanodots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working function and performance comparative of BCDs-Based Photosensitizers in DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: The working mechanism of a DSSC, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 10 , highlights the stacked layers of materials used in this study. In this system, n-type semiconductor TiO2 acts as the electron transport medium. Photosensitizers, such as BCDs (DMF) or BCDs (H2O2), are adsorbed onto the semiconductor, absorbing photons from incident light, enhancing light absorption and facilitating photogenerated electron production.
Upon sunlight exposure, BCDs (DMF) or BCDs (H2O2) molecules excite electrons from their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO).

These excited electrons are then injected into the conduction band of TiO2, which lies below the excited state of the BCDs. The injected electrons travel through the TiO2 layer, diffuse into the conducting fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) layer, and flow through the external circuit, completing the charge separation and transport process. Simultaneously, the reduction process occurs at the platinum (Pt) counter electrode, where electrons from the external circuit are transferred back to the electrolyte, ensuring continuous operation.

The nanocarbons, synthesized from various sources using different methods, are compared and evaluated as sensitizers in DSSC systems, as shown in Fig. 11 and Table 5 . The raw carbon precursors in previous studies include chemical carbon sources such as urea, citric acid, and sodium citrate dihydrate, as well as biomass-derived carbon sources like chitin, chitosan, and glucose, which yield CQDs, CDs, and nitrogen-doped N-CDs, respectively. This work is the first step to utilizing pyrolysis oil as a carbon precursor for producing carbon nanomaterials used as sensitizers in DSSC applications. As a result, pyrolysis oil can effectively serve as a carbon source for producing nanocarbon dots, significantly enhancing the efficiency of Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs) when used as a sensitizer. The power conversion efficiency (PCE%) of the bio-oil sensitizer is higher than that of other carbon dots-based sensitizers produced from other natural biomass and chemical sources.

The properties and functional bonding of carbon nanomaterials depend on the raw carbon source and synthesis method used. Thus, this factor has a critical role when sensitizing carbon nanodots from pyrolysis bio-oil. During the synthesis process, various methods such as hydrothermal and oxidation methods, as well as solvents like DMF and H2O2, facilitate the beneficial doping of heteroatoms, specifically nitrogen, and oxygen, into the bio-oil-derived carbon dots (BCDs). These surface modifications affected their structure. This process further enhances the overall performance of the Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs). The BCDs produced with DMF resulted in a structure of carbon quantum dots, while the BCDs produced with H2O2 led to a structure of carbon nanodots. Based on the results, the carbon quantum dots from BCDs (DMF) demonstrated higher efficiency compared to the carbon nanodots from BCDs (H2O2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: This study demonstrates the successful conversion of cornstalk-derived pyrolysis oil into high-value biochar through atmospheric distillation, producing a carbon-rich precursor for advanced nanomaterials. Two types of bio-oil-derived carbon nanomaterials (BCDs) were synthesized using distinct methods and solvents: BCDs (DMF) and BCDs (H2O2). BCDs (DMF) exhibit characteristics similar to Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), with well-defined crystalline graphitic domains, smaller particle sizes (3.1 nm), nitrogen-rich functional groups, and green fluorescence. In contrast, BCDs (H2O2) resemble Carbon Nanodots (CNDs), displaying larger particle sizes (4.4 nm), amorphous structures, oxygen-dominated surfaces, and blue fluorescence. The narrower band gap of BCDs (DMF) (2.2 eV) enables superior light harvesting, efficient charge transfer, and reduced electron recombination compared to BCDs (H2O2) (2.8 eV). As photosensitizers in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), BCDs (DMF) significantly outperformed BCDs (H2O2), achieving a 59.2 % increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) and a 47.7 % enhancement in short-circuit current density (Jsc). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirmed that BCDs (DMF) based DSSC devices exhibited lower recombination rates, longer electron lifetimes, and faster electron transport, resulting in reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct). Thus, these findings underscore the potential of pyrolysis oil-derived carbon nanomaterials for next-generation photovoltaics, with improved charge separation and electron transport. Future work should optimize synthesis processes, scale production, and explore applications in sustainable energy and optoelectronics.
